one_line_takeaway: A GDPR-grounded position paper arguing that synthetic-data anonymity claims must be assessed at the level of the trained generative model using state-of-the-art privacy attacks (MIA/AIA/reconstruction/differencing), concluding that Differential Privacy can meet regulatory anonymization standards while similarity-based privacy metrics (e.g., DCR) cannot.
key_finding: The paper's central claim is that (i) synthetic tabular data produced without any formal privacy mechanism does not satisfy GDPR-style anonymization sufficiency, because motivated black-box adversaries can run membership-inference, attribute-inference, and reconstruction attacks against the underlying generative model (citing, e.g., attribute-inference success 'in some cases with up to 95% probability' from cited work [ReconSynth/7], and reconstruction from repeated sampling [20,22,40] -- these are numbers reported in the cited attack papers, not new measurements by this paper); (ii) DP-trained generative models can, when properly configured, mitigate all three regulatory risks (singling out, linkability, inferences) both theoretically (via DP's formal worst-case guarantee and post-processing property) and empirically (via cited MIA/AIA/reconstruction studies showing reduced attack success under DP); and (iii) SBPMs (distance-to-closest-record-style metrics) systematically fail to provide adequate protection because they lack a formal adversarial model, only measure average-case rather than worst-case leakage, provide no plausible deniability, and have been shown in cited work (ReconSyn [58]) to be defeatable via simple black-box reconstruction and inference attacks using only a handful of API calls.
core_contribution: This is a conceptual/legal-technical position paper (not an empirical attack or defense paper). Georgi Ganev and Emiliano De Cristofaro argue that anonymity claims about ML-generated synthetic tabular data should be assessed at the level of the trained generative model rather than at the level of a single released dataset, reflecting how modern synthetic-data products actually work (retrainable, queryable models producing unlimited datasets, not a one-off dataset release). The paper's three concrete contributions are: (1) an interpretation of the GDPR/UK-GDPR's three anonymization-sufficiency risks -- singling out, linkability, and inferences (per Article 29 WP and the ICO) -- in terms of generative-model behavior; (2) an explicit mapping of each regulatory risk to a class of privacy attack from the ML privacy literature: singling out -> differencing attacks, linkability -> membership inference attacks (MIA), inferences -> attribute inference attacks (AIA), plus reconstruction attacks as an attack type that subsumes all three risks (summarized in the paper's Table 1, which lists specific named attacks from prior work -- e.g. GroundHog, DOMIAS, AuditSynth, MIDST, ReconSynth, RAP-Rank, ReconSyn -- as illustrative instances, not attacks the authors themselves run); and (3) an argument, structured around eight privacy-related criteria (Table 2: adversarial model, privacy guarantees, privacy analysis, privacy risk, plausible deniability, privacy subject, information leakage, privacy expenditure), that Differential Privacy (DP) can in principle satisfy all three regulatory risks and support an anonymity claim, whereas Similarity-Based Privacy Metrics (SBPMs, e.g. Distance to Closest Record) cannot, because they lack a defined adversarial model, provide only average-case empirical guarantees, and have been shown in cited prior work to be defeatable by simple attacks.
threat_model: The paper does not run its own attacks; it adopts the 'motivated intruder' test from UK/EU regulatory guidance (a competent, resourced attacker with access to publicly available data/tools and the model's outputs) and maps this to standard ML privacy-attack threat settings drawn from cited work: black-box (API-only query access to the trained generative model), white-box (full access to model parameters/architecture, and in some cited works, intermediate training steps), and no-box (access only to the released synthetic dataset, no model access). It distinguishes attacker side information (knowledge of the training algorithm, access to reference/similar datasets, partial knowledge of a target record) across the four attack families it discusses: differencing attacks (singling out), membership inference attacks (linkability), attribute inference attacks (inferences), and reconstruction attacks (all three risks jointly).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: DP implementation and privacy-budget selection remain technically challenging for non-experts and introduce computational overhead; the degree to which DP mitigates identifiability risk is highly context-dependent, so no universal privacy-budget guideline is offered; the paper explicitly scopes itself to only the anonymization/identifiability strand of GDPR and defers other GDPR aspects (purpose limitation, consent, data minimization, governance) to future work. Additional limitations evident from the text: the paper is purely conceptual/synthesis work -- it does not itself implement or run any of the cited attacks, so its risk-severity claims (e.g., the 95% AIA figure) are inherited from cited studies with their own (unstated-in-this-paper) datasets, model types, and assumptions rather than being independently verified here; Table 1's attack list is illustrative/example-based rather than a systematic or exhaustive survey; and the DP-vs-SBPM comparison in Table 2 is qualitative/criteria-based rather than a head-to-head quantitative benchmark.
